steps.title: Fastest check: compare a table or graph
steps.content: For an equivalent-expression question, enter the original expression and the answer choices in a calculator table or graph. The correct choice must match for several inputs, not just one. The matching choice is D, $\frac{36a \left({k}^{2}+1\right)}{k}$.

steps.title: Algebra check
steps.content: Use the work below to confirm the identity for every allowed input. This matters because one random matching input is only a quick screen, not a proof that two expressions are always equivalent.

steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: Rewrite the second term with denominator k: $36ak = \frac{36ak^{2}}{k}$.

choices.elimination:
• This would be correct only if $36ak = \frac{36a}{k}$, i.e., if $k = \frac{1}{k}$, which is only true when $k=1$.
  • For general $k \gt 0$, the second term is $\frac{36ak^{2}}{k}$, not $\frac{36a}{k}$.
  • Eliminated because the $k^{2}$ factor in the numerator is dropped.

• This simplifies to $72ak$, which equals $2 \times 36ak$ — it ignores the $\frac{36a}{k}$ term entirely.
  • Both terms must be combined, not just one of them doubled.
  • Eliminated because the first term $\frac{36a}{k}$ is absent.

• Expanding: $\frac{36a(k+1)}{k} = \frac{36ak + 36a}{k} = 36a + \frac{36a}{k}$.
  • This equals the original only if $36ak = 36a$, i.e., $k = 1$. It doesn't hold for general $k$.
  • Eliminated because the correct factor is $(k^{2}+1)$, not $(k+1)$.

• Convert the second term: $36ak = \frac{36ak^{2}}{k}$.
  • Add: $\frac{36a}{k} + \frac{36ak^{2}}{k} = \frac{36a(1+k^{2})}{k} = \frac{36a(k^{2}+1)}{k}$.
  • This matches exactly. That confirms D is correct.
